coredata.dc:title: Surficial amino groups coupling induced concentration-dependent fluorescence and fluorescence quantum yield of nitrogen-dopped carbon quantum dots via efficient charge transfer
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Fengjiao
Liu, Zhaoshuang
Sui, Shuxin
Huang, Kai
Yang, Yang
Chen, Zhen
Yin, Hongming
coredata.dc:description: Modification of surficial functional groups among carbon quantum dots (CQDs) has been considered an efficient approach to regulate the fluorescence emission of CQDs. However, the mechanism of how surficial functional groups affect fluorescence is vague which fundamentally limits the further applications of CQDs. Here we report the concentration-dependent fluorescence and fluorescence quantum yield of nitrogen-dopped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs). At high concentrations (≥0.188 g/L), fluorescence redshift occurs accompanied with decrease in fluorescence quantum yield. Fluorescence excitation spectra and HOMO-LUMO energy gaps calculations show that energy levels of excited states of N-CQDs are relocated via the coupling of surficial amino groups among N-CQDs. Furthermore, electron density difference maps and broadened fluorescence spectra obtained from both experimental measurement and theoretical calculation further confirm that the coupling of surficial amino groups dominates the fluorescence property and verify the formation of charge-transfer state of N-CQDs complex at high concentrations which provides pathways for efficient charge transfer. Given that charge-transfer state induced fluorescence loss and fluorescence spectra broadening are the typical characteristics of organic molecules, CQDs exhibit the optical properties of both quantum dots and organic molecules.

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Concentration-dependent FL and FLQY of N-CQDs
Mechanism of concentration-dependent FL of N-CQDs.

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: State Key Laboratory of Molecular Reaction Dynamics

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support from the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (Grant No. 3132022197) and open fund of the state key laboratory of molecular reaction dynamics in DICP, CAS.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of as-synthesized N-CQDs, inset is size distribution of N-CQDs; (b) Normalized absorption (Abs), fluorescence (FL) (excited @331 nm) and fluorescence excitation (FLE) (monitored @ 412 nm) spectra of N-CQDs.

X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) of as-synthesized N-CQDs (a) and corresponded high resolution XPS of C1s (b), O1s (c) and N1s (d). In (b)(c)(d), raw data are plotted in solid black lines and cumulative fitting curves are plotted in dashed red lines. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Fluorescence spectra (a), fluorescence peak wavelength (b) and fluorescence quantum yield (c) of N-CQDs with concentrations ranging from 0.012 g/L to 6 g/L.

Gaussian fit results of FL curves with different concentrations of N-CQDs ranging from 0.012 g/L to 6 g/L. (a) FL and Gaussian fit curve of N-CQDs with concentration of 0.012 g/L, raw data are plotted in black dots, cumulative fitting curve is plotted in yellow line; Corresponding fitted FL peak wavelength (b), FL peak height (c) and FL peak integration (d) of N-CQDs. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) FLE spectra of N-CQDs with different concentrations ranging from 0.012 g/L to 6 g/L which are monitored at the strongest FL emission wavelength for each concentration; Corresponding peak wavelength (b) and peak intensity (c) of the strongest FLE peaks in (a).

Gaussian fit results of FLE curves with different concentrations of N-CQDs ranging from 0.012 g/L to 6 g/L. (a) FLE and Gaussian fit curve of N-CQDs with concentration of 0.012 g/L, raw data are plotted in solid black line, cumulative fitting curve is plotted in light blue line; Corresponding fitted FLE peak wavelength (b), FLE peak height (c) and FLE peak integration (d) of N-CQDs. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Top and side views of optimized configurations of ground-state (GS) and excited-state (ES) NH2-CQDs and COOH-CQDs at low and high concentrations which are denoted as NH2-CQDs-1, NH2-CQDs-2, COOH-CQDs-1 and COOH-CQDs-2, respectively. Grey atoms represent carbon, blue atoms represent nitrogen, white atoms represent hydrogen and red atoms represent oxygen. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Molecular orbital diagrams and calculated HOMO-LUMO energy levels for NH2-CQDs and COOH-CQDs at low and high concentrations. The values and images of HOMO and LUMO were obtained at B3LYP/6-311G (d) level.

Electron density difference maps (EDDMs) between ground state and first excited state of NH2-CQDs and COOH-CQDs at low and high concentrations with B3LYP/6-31G(d) level of calculation. Yellow area indicates the electron density loss and the blue color indicates the electron density gain in transition. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Calculated fluorescence spectra of (a) NH2-CQDs and (b) COOH-CQDs at low and high concentrations are colored in blue and orange lines, respectively. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

head.ce:title: Surficial amino groups coupling induced concentration-dependent fluorescence and fluorescence quantum yield of nitrogen-dopped carbon quantum dots via efficient charge transfer

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Science, Dalian Maritime University, Dalian, Liaoning 116026, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Science, Dalian Maritime University, Dalian, Liaoning 116026, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Molecular Reaction Dynamics, Dalian Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Dalian 116023, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Molecular Reaction Dynamics

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Molecular Reaction Dynamics, Dalian Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Dalian 116023, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Concentration-dependent fluorescence and fluorescence quantum yield of nitrogen-dopped carbon quantum dots which is induced by coupling of surficial amino groups which forms charge-transfer state.

Modification of surficial functional groups among carbon quantum dots (CQDs) has been considered an efficient approach to regulate the fluorescence emission of CQDs. However, the mechanism of how surficial functional groups affect fluorescence is vague which fundamentally limits the further applications of CQDs. Here we report the concentration-dependent fluorescence and fluorescence quantum yield of nitrogen-dopped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs). At high concentrations (≥0.188 g/L), fluorescence redshift occurs accompanied with decrease in fluorescence quantum yield. Fluorescence excitation spectra and HOMO-LUMO energy gaps calculations show that energy levels of excited states of N-CQDs are relocated via the coupling of surficial amino groups among N-CQDs. Furthermore, electron density difference maps and broadened fluorescence spectra obtained from both experimental measurement and theoretical calculation further confirm that the coupling of surficial amino groups dominates the fluorescence property and verify the formation of charge-transfer state of N-CQDs complex at high concentrations which provides pathways for efficient charge transfer. Given that charge-transfer state induced fluorescence loss and fluorescence spectra broadening are the typical characteristics of organic molecules, CQDs exhibit the optical properties of both quantum dots and organic molecules.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have been intensively studied in recent years due to their outstanding optical properties, water solubility, low cost, low toxicity and good biocompatibility which make CQDs great potential competitor to conventional semiconductor nanomaterials [1–4]. As a result, CQDs have been widely used in light-emitting diodes (LEDs), biomedicine, fluorescence sensors, ion detection, food safety, etc. [5–9]. Among a series of characteristics of CQDs, their fluorescence (FL) emission property has gained the most attention since FL emission is the fundamental process for the wide variety of technical applications such as LEDs, ion detection, bioimaging and et al. [1,10,11].

During the preparation of CQDs based on bottom-up approach, carbon source is usually chosen as various organic compounds such as citric acid (CA) [12], glucose [13], L-ascorbic acid [14], dopamine [15], etc. As a result, CQDs do not end with orderly arranged carbon atoms and they are commonly decorated by a variety of surficial functional groups like carboxyl (–COOH), hydroxyl (–OH), amino (–NH2), etc. As a nanomaterial, CQDs show the size-dependent FL due to quantum confinement [3,16]. Meanwhile, functional groups distributed among CQDs can also dramatically change FL [17,18]. This provides a much facile and efficient approach to regulate CQDs FL due to the relatively easy surficial functionalization and passivation processes of CQDs [19,20]. It has been found that both amount and species of functional groups could relocate excite states and significantly change FL emission wavelength of CQDs [21]. However, mechanism of surficial functional groups regulating FL of CQDs is still unclear. To facilitate the applications of controllable fluorescence-emitting CQDs via functionalization, extensive fundamental researches are in urgent need considering the abundant species of surficial functional groups.

In this work, we investigate the concentration-dependent FL emission of nitrogen-dopped CQDs (N-CQDs) combining experimental and theoretical methods. For N-CQDs with the average diameter of ∼3.2 nm, at high concentrations which is greater than or equal to 0.188 g/L, the redshift of FL peak and decrease of fluorescence quantum yield (FLQY) begin as concentration increases. This is explained by strong interaction between surficial amino groups (–NH2) among N-CQDs which build pathways for efficient charge transfer between N-CQDs within CQDs complex at high concentrations. The fluorescence excitation (FLE) spectra and the HOMO-LUMO energy gaps further confirm the relocation of excited states of N-CQDs via coupled surficial amino groups. Furthermore, the results of electron density difference maps (EDDMs) and broadened FL spectra obtained from both experimental measurement and theoretical calculation verify the formation of charge-transfer state of N-CQDs complex. Given that charge-transfer state induced FL loss and FL peak broadening are the typical characters of organic molecules, therefore, N-CQDs exhibit the properties of both quantum dots and organic molecules.

The UV–Vis absorption spectrum in this work is measured using Shimadzu UV3600. Steady-state FL/FLE spectra were measured using Hitachi F4600. FLQY was obtained by comparing the FL peak integration with the fluorescence standard reference material of quinine sulfate (QS) under the same experimental conditions [22,23].

Density functional theory (DFT) and time-dependent density functional theory (TDDFT) methods were used by Gaussian 09 programs for ground state and excited state of all CQDs systems, respectively. For all theoretical calculations, functional of B3LYP, basis set of 6-311G(d) and solvation mode of vacuum were used.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image of N-CQDs (Fig. 1 a) indicates the uniform sphere shape of N-CQDs with an average diameter of ∼3.2 nm. Materials and CQDs sample preparation can be found in supporting information (SI1 and SI2). X-Ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) result shown in Fig. 2 a suggests that elements in N-CQDs include carbon, oxygen and nitrogen. Considering the carbon source of N-CQDs is CA, oxygen-containing functional groups inevitably exist among N-CQDs. To further figure out the bond structure of such N-CQDs, the high-resolution scan spectra of C1s, O1s and N1s were measured. From Fig. 2b, the strong peak at a binding energy of 284.8 eV confirms the domain sp2 C–C in the graphitic structure of N-CQDs [24–26]. The peak at a binding energy of 286.0 eV corresponds to C–O and C–N species in N-CQDs.[27] The peak at a binding energy of 288.1 eV could be attributed to C=O group on the N-CQDs surface [27,28]. For O1s spectrum displayed in Fig. 2c, two peaks show up at the binding energies of 531.3 eV and 532.8 eV which could be assigned to C=O and O–H, respectively [28–30]. The N1s high-resolution spectrum (Fig. 2d) shows a strong peak at the binding energies of 399.8 eV indicating the predominant C-NH2 structure of doped nitrogen in CQDs [31,32]. The other weak peak appears at 401.8 eV suggests the small amount of C-NH3 + structure [32]. The XPS results suggest that as-synthesized N-CQDs have the uniform carbon-based core structure and the most nitrogen dopped in CQDs exists as amino (–NH2) among the surface of N-CQDs. Detailed XPS fitting results can be found in supporting information (SI3). Furthermore, fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra measurement of N-CQDs were also conducted to better understand their graphitic structure, elemental composition and surficial functional groups. As shown in Fig. S1, compared with CA and urea, characteristic peaks of C–C, C-N, N–H, C=O and C-O show up which further confirm the surficial functional groups of N-CQDs as discussed above.

To characterize optical property, the absorption, FL and FLE spectra of as-synthesized N-CQDs were measured and the results are shown in Fig. 1b. The absorption at far UV range below 260 nm can be attributed to the π–π* transition of C=C as core structure of N-CQDs, and the absorption peak centered at 331 nm can be assigned to the n–π* transition at nitrogen doped sites which are surficial amino groups by the surface state among N-CQDs [33–35]. Moreover, the separated and barely overlapping peak at 331 nm indicates that surficial amino groups of N-CQDs provide a single surface state energy level with a bandgap of 3.75 eV. Under 331 nm irradiation, N-CQDs present strong bule FL at 412 nm (3.01 eV) which can be ascribed to FL emissive site of surficial amino groups of CQDs [36,37].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Concentration-dependent FL and FLQY of N-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: FL spectra of N-CQDs with different concentrations ranging from 0.012 g/L to 6 g/L were measured and shown in Fig. 3 a. It can be found that FL firstly gets stronger and then decreases with increase of concentrations. In our experiment, the strongest FL of N-CQDs appears at the concentration of 0.188 g/L. The comparison of FL spectra in flat view can be found in SI5. Except FL intensity, FL peak wavelength is relevant to N-CQDs concentration as well. As shown in Fig. 3b, with the concentration of 0.012–0.188 g/L, FL peaks locate at similar wavelength around 413 nm. However, when concentration of N-CQDs is greater than 0.188 g/L, FL peak occurs redshift and reaches to 453 nm at concentration of 6 g/L. FLQY of N-CQDs with different concentrations was also measured and shown in Fig. 3c. FLQY results present consistent trend of FL peak wavelength result in Fig. 3b which is FLQY shows the uniform result of ∼74.9% at concentrations below or equal to 0.188 g/L. Then FLQY decreases to pretty low value (1.9%) as concentration rises to 6 g/L.

To better understand concentration-dependent FL emission of N-CQDs, FL spectra with all concentrations are fitted using Gaussian fit method and results are displayed in Fig. 4 . As shown in Fig. 4a, at concentration of 0.012 g/L, three fitted Gaussian FL peaks appear at 401.4 nm (FL-P1), 423.0 nm (FL-P2) and 452.0 nm (FL-P3), respectively. Fitting curves at other concentrations are shown in SI6. For fitted FL peak wavelength which can be found in Fig. 4b, FL-P1, FL-P2 and FL-P3 show the same trend which is being unchanged with the concentration below 0.188 g/L, as concentration increases starting from 0.188 g/L, redshift begins for all three fitted FL peaks and the higher concentration yields greater redshift rate. Eventually, at concentration of 6 g/L, FL-P1, FL-P2 and FL-P3 have shifted to 430.1 nm, 452.6 nm and 485.6 nm, respectively. Furthermore, fitted FL peak height and peak integration are also analyzed and shown in Fig. 4(c-d). Within concentration range from 0.012 g/L to 0.094 g/L, all fitted FL peak height and peak integration are both linearly dependent on the concentration. As concentration increases from 0.094 g/L, FL peak height and FL peak integration firstly grow slowly (@ 0.188 g/L), then start to decay and the rate of decay increases with the increasing concentration.

The concentration-dependent property of FL suggests that surficial functional groups among N-CQDs are the true FL emission sites since excited core structure inside CQDs is barely affected by surrounding CQDs and commonly present concentration-independent FL emission. At low concentrations, interactions between N-CQDs are negligible and as a result, FL emission depends on the individual N-CQD. This is supported by FL spectra results at concentrations below 0.094 g/L: the uniform FLQY and FL peak wavelength indicate that original FL emission of single N-CQD dominates the overall FL spectra at such concentrations and linear growth of FL peak height and FL peak area with the increasing concentration further demonstrate that increased FL emission purely derive from increase in numbers of N-CQDs. At high concentrations of N-CQDs (≥0.188 g/L), redshift of both experimentally measured and fitted FL peaks is commonly caused by strong coupling of surficial functional groups which usually leads to significant increase of non-irradiation transition rate which is supported by FLQY decreasing [38,39].

FLE spectra at different concentrations are measured and results are shown in Fig. 5 . In FLE spectra, emission wavelength was monitored at the strongest FL peak wavelength for each concentration. From Fig. 5a, at concentration of 0.012 g/L, two FLE peaks show up at around 255 nm and 347 nm which are ascribed to the slight π-π* transition and predominate n-π* transition, respectively. With increase of concentration, these two peaks both go stronger and then decay which is accompanied with peak splitting into two for the stronger FLE peak (@∼347 nm) starting from the concentration of 0.188 g/L. The comparison of FLE spectra in flat view can be found in SI7. Being similar to FL spectra results, the strongest FLE peaks position occurs redshift as the increase of concentration (≥0.188 g/L) which is shown in Fig. 5b and the corresponding FLE peak intensity are plotted in Fig. 5c.

To further understand how concentration affects radiative excited states, FLE peaks of N-CQDs at different concentrations are fitted using multiple Gaussian peak fit method and results are displayed in Fig. 6 . At concentration of 0.012 g/L, the Gaussian fit result yields four peaks which are located at 251 nm (FLE-P1), 334 nm (FLE-P2), 353 nm (FLE-P3) and 368 nm (FLE-P4), respectively (Fig. 6a). This result suggests that multiple surface excited states are existing. FLE fitting curves of N-CQDs at other concentrations can be found in SI8. As shown in Fig. 6 (b-d), at relatively low concentrations (≤0.094 g/L), fitted FLE peak positions are invariable as concentration increases, both fitted FLE peak height and peak integration show linear positive correlation to concentration. At high concentrations (＞0.094 g/L) of N-CQDs, FLE-P1, FLE-P3 and FLE-P4 exhibit redshift while FLE-P2 exhibits blueshift as concentration increases. Furthermore, fitted FLE peak height and peak integration reach the top at concentration of 0.188 g/L and then start to decay with increase of concentration. Specially, at concentrations of 3 g/L and 6 g/L, more FLE peaks appear and eventually five and six fitted FLE peaks are obtained, respectively.

FLE spectra results of N-CQDs at relatively low concentrations (≤0.094 g/L) correspond to FL spectra results which verify that surface excited states for FL emission is determined by individual N-CQD with negligible interactions between N-CQDs. As N-CQDs solution are condensed, FLE spectra results further demonstrate that the enhanced interactions between N-CQDs dramatically relocate energy levels of multiple surficial excited states which present redshift and blueshift of FLE peaks. Specially, at high concentrations (≥3 g/L), it can be found that fitted FLE results present complex trend comparing with those at lower concentrations. This could be caused by complicated energy levels distribution of surface excited states via strong coupling between surficial functional groups.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of concentration-dependent FL of N-CQDs.
ce:section.ce:para: To figure out the mechanism of concentration-dependent FL property of N-CQDs, theoretical calculations were conducted. From FL and FLE spectra results discussed above, surficial functional groups among N-CQDs are the critical factor which controls the FL wavelength and FLQY. Based on characterization results of N-CQDs, aminos (–NH2) are the main surficial functional group among N-CQDs. Herein, we propose that interaction between surficial amino groups among N-CQDs accounts for concentration-dependent FL and FLQY. Considering CA as carbon source of N-CQDs, carboxyl (–COOH) is inevitable distributed among N-CQDs. To eliminate potential influence of carboxyl on concentration-dependent FL characteristic, investigations of CQDs containing carboxyl are also conducted.

Single CQD and coupled CQDs for both NH2-CQDs and COOH-CQDs were chosen for theoretical calculations to simulate CQDs under low and high concentration conditions which are denoted as NH2-CQDs-1, NH2-CQDs-2, COOH-CQDs-1 and COOH-CQDs-2, respectively. Configurations of ground-state (GS) and excited-state (ES) NH2-CQDs and COOH-CQDs were optimized and results are shown in Fig. 7 . It can be found that, under low concentration, configurations of NH2-CQDs-1 and COOH-CQDs-1 at ground-state and excited-state all show flat carbon core structures and there is no change in configuration between ground-state and excited-state CQDs. Under high concentration, for both configurations of ground-state and excited-state CQDs, all individual CQDs involved in coupled CQDs still maintain the flat structure, however, coupled two CQDs exhibit dihedral angle of 57.62° (NH2-CQDs-2) and 12.23° (COOH-CQDs-2) at ground state. After excitation, excited NH2-CQDs-2 shows significant twist between two coupled NH2-CQDs and the variation of the dihedral angle between excited two coupled NH2-CQDs is 17.22° comparing with ground-state configuration, whereas excited COOH-CQDs-2 shows barely change in configuration comparing with that in ground-state. Optimized configuration results reveal that interactions between surficial amino groups among CQDs have significant influence on relative position within the coupled CQDs after excitation while surficial carboxyl groups show negligible effect on configuration.

The highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) energies levels have been widely used in CQDs systems because they have a more accurate correspondence with the photon absorption process [40,41]. The calculated HOMO and LUMO results are shown in Fig. 8 . For NH2-CQDs, the coupled CQDs have a lower LUMO energy level and a higher HOMO energy level comparing with single CQD which finally changes HOMO-LUMO energy gap from 3.72 eV to 3.38 eV. This result accounts for the redshift of FLE wavelength for N-CQDs. For COOH-CQDs, comparing with single COOH-CQD, the coupled COOH-CQDs shows almost unchanged HOMO/LUMO energy levels and yield negligible change of HOMO-LUMO energy gap (0.03 eV). This result indicates that surficial carboxyl groups have less impact on excitation process which results in concentration-independent FL emission. The HOMO-LUMO calculation results demonstrate that, comparing with carboxyl groups, interactions between surficial amino groups can dramatically reduce HOMO-LUMO energy gap at high concentrations and are the critical factor resulting in concentration-dependent FL and FLE spectra.

To illustrate the electron redistribution during excitation process, the electron density difference maps (EDDMs) between ground state (S0) and first excited state (S1) of NH2-CQDs and COOH-CQDs at low and high concentrations are calculated and results are shown in Fig. 9 . At low concentrations, as shown in Fig. 9a, the S0 → S1 excitation of NH2 CQDs-1 shows n-π* transition characteristic at surficial amino groups which is consistent with UV–Vis absorption spectra of N-CQDs in Fig. 1b. At high concentrations, as shown in Fig. 9b, the excitation process of coupled NH2-CQDs-2 involves significant charge transfer across two NH2-CQDs where the coupled amino groups offer the charge transfer pathway. In contrast to NH2-CQDs, as shown in Fig. 9c, the single COOH-CQDs-1 shows π–π* transition of C=C and the electron density is mainly redistributed within the core structure of CQDs. For coupled COOH-CQDs-2 shown in Fig. 9d, it can be found that coupled carboxyl has absolutely no impact on excitation process of COOH-CQDs. Based on EDDMs results, we propose the mechanism of concentration-dependent FL and FLQY of N-CQDs that coupled amino groups among N-CQDs surface at high concentrations provide pathway for efficient charge transfer which forms the charge-transfer state and the energy level of such charge-transfer state of N-CQDs complex is much lower than the first excited state of single N-CQDs which leads to redshift of FL. Moreover, the charge-transfer state has more non-radiative transition pathways which lead to the decrease of FLQY.

Furthermore, calculated FL spectra of NH2-CQDs and COOH-CQDs are displayed in Fig. 10 . From Fig. 10a, calculated FL peaks of single NH2-CQDs-1 and coupled NH2-CQDs-2 appear at 432.4 nm and 600.8 nm which agree well with experimentally measured FL spectra of N-CQDs. For COOH-CQDs, as shown in Fig. 10b, almost no redshift occurs and calculated FL peaks show up at 434.8 nm and 436.0 nm for single COOH-CQDs-1 and coupled COOH-CQDs-2, respectively. It is important to note that, the coupled NH2-CQDs-2 shows an enhanced width of FL spectra with full width at half maximum (FWHM) of 218.4 nm comparing with the single NH2-CQDs-1 with FWHM of 100.8 nm. Moreover, the experimentally measured FL spectra of N-CQDs also exhibit broader FL emission as concentration increases which is shown in SI9. Based on previous reports, the redshift and broadening of FL emission are typical characteristics of intermolecular and intramolecular charge-transfer state in organic molecular systems [42,43,44,45]. Therefore, both calculated and experimentally measured FL spectra verify the mechanism of charge-transfer state induced concentration-dependent FL of N-CQDs. Meanwhile, in this work, N-CQDs present the optical properties of both quantum dots and organic molecules.

To further verify the key role of surficial amino groups in concentration-dependent FL and FLQY instead of carboxyl, the carboxyl decorated CQDs were synthesized and FL spectra with different concentrations were measured (SI10). From Fig. S7, FL intensity increases as concentration increases from 0.094 g/L to 6 g/L with a fixed FL peak at ∼470 nm. The decline in FL growth at 6 g/L is mainly attributed to photon blocking from part COOH-CQDs in such condensed solution which reduces absorption intensity.

We report the concentration-dependent FL and FLQY of N-CQDs. At relatively low concentrations (<0.188 g/L), FL and FLQY are determined by individual N-CQD. As concentration increases (≥0.188 g/L), the FL emission wavelength occurs redshift and FLQY is dramatically reduced. The FLE spectra and calculated HOMO-LUMO energy gaps reveal that surficial amino groups among N-CQDs are the critical sites for FL emission and strong coupling of amino groups can significantly change excited-state location of N-CQDs which accounts for the redshift of FL. Furthermore, the EDDMs and broadened both measured and calculated FL spectra at high concentrations reveal that charge-transfer state has been formed via coupled amino groups which build pathways for efficient charge transfer within the N-CQDs complex. This is consistent with optical property of organic molecules, as a result, amino groups decorated CQDs exhibit the optical properties of both quantum dots and organic molecules.

Fengjiao Zhao: Conceptualization, Investigation, Data curation, Formal analysis, Funding acquisition, Methodology, Project administration, Resources, Software, Supervision, Visualization, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing. Hongming Yin: Project administration, Supervision, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support from the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (Grant No. 3132022197) and open fund of the state key laboratory of molecular reaction dynamics in DICP, CAS.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Materials, sample preparation, XPS spectra fitting results, FL spectra of N-CQDs with different concentrations, Gaussian fit of FL Curves at different concentrations of N-CQDs, FLE spectra of N-CQDs with different concentrations, Gaussian fit of FLE curves at different concentrations of N-CQDs, FWHM of N-CQDs FL spectra at different concentrations, FL spectra of carboxyl groups (-COOH) decorated CQDs at different concentrations can be found in Supporting Information. Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2023.122542.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon quantum dots and their applications
Chem. Soc. Rev.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Lim, S. Y.; Shen, W.; Gao, Z., Carbon quantum dots and their applications. Chemical Society Reviews 2015, 44 (1), 362-381.

Adv. Colloid Interface Sci.
